title: Contact
text: I welcome professional inquiries, applied research collaborations, and opportunities in industry, government, or academia that leverage my expertise in mathematical biology, infectious disease modeling, and ecological systems analysis. I am particularly interested in projects that apply quantitative modeling and data-driven decision-making to address real-world challenges in public health, environmental management, and policy development.

title: Teaching
text: My teaching approach emphasizes active learning, practical application, and the integration of real-world data into the classroom. Drawing on my research in climate-driven mosquito population dynamics, I aim to equip students with the analytical skills and problem-solving strategies needed to address complex questions in mathematics, biology, and data science. I place particular emphasis on helping students connect mathematical theory with computational implementation and real-world applications.

title: Research
text:
My research focuses on developing and analyzing mechanistic mathematical models of mosquito population dynamics under changing environmental conditions, including temperature, rainfall, and photoperiod. These models are designed to capture how seasonal forcing and climate variability shape mosquito life cycles, population structure, and disease risk.
I am currently a Postdoctoral Research Fellow at York University, where I extend this work to study climate-driven mosquito dynamics in Canada, including regional comparisons and the interaction of environmental drivers across diverse climatic settings.

title: Research
text:
My research focuses on developing and analyzing mechanistic mathematical models of mosquito population dynamics under changing environmental conditions, including temperature, rainfall, and photoperiod. These models are designed to capture how seasonal forcing and climate variability shape mosquito life cycles, population structure, and disease risk.
I am currently a Postdoctoral Research Fellow at York University, where I extend this work to study climate-driven mosquito dynamics in Canada, including regional comparisons and the interaction of environmental drivers across diverse climatic settings.

title: Research
text:
Modeling Approaches
I develop and analyze stage-structured dynamical systems, primarily using ordinary differential equations (ODEs) to represent mosquito life stages (eggs, larvae, pupae, active adults, and diapausing adults). These models incorporate key biological processes such as density dependence, temperature- and rainfall-dependent development, and diapause dynamics.
To capture environmental variability, I integrate both deterministic seasonal forcing and stochastic representations of rainfall, allowing for realistic simulation of climate-driven systems. Numerical simulations are implemented in R using reproducible workflows and efficient solvers.

title: Research
text:
Applications
My modeling framework is used to investigate:

The effects of climate variability and climate change on mosquito abundance and population structure.

The timing, duration, and intensity of mosquito activity, including shifts in peak abundance and active season length.

The interaction between temperature and precipitation in shaping mosquito dynamics across regions.

The implications for vector-borne disease risk and control strategies under different environmental scenarios.

title: Research
text:
Analytical Tools
To assess model behavior and robustness, I apply a range of quantitative methods, including:

Global sensitivity analysis using Latin hypercube sampling (LHS) and partial rank correlation coefficients (PRCC)

Uncertainty analysis to quantify the impact of parameter and input variability

Scenario-based simulations to evaluate alternative climate and intervention conditions

Data-driven model integration, combining observed climate data with mechanistic models

I use R extensively for simulation, statistical analysis, and visualization (e.g., ggplot2), and develop reproducible pipelines for managing and processing climate data.

title: Research
text:
Code and Data
The code and data supporting my research are openly available on GitHub:
- Mosquito Dynamics Model
- Climate Data Analysis
- Sensitivity Analysis
- climecol R package on GitHub

title: Joseph Baafi
text:
Joseph Baafi, PhD


Postdoctoral Fellow in Mathematical Biology Climate-Driven Ecological & Infectious Disease Modeling


Download CV GitHub LinkedIn Email me





Welcome 👋


I am a mathematical biologist and Postdoctoral Fellow at York University, specializing in climate-driven models of mosquito population dynamics. My research integrates temperature, rainfall, and photoperiod to better understand vector ecology, overwintering dynamics, and the effects of environmental change on mosquito abundance and disease risk.


I completed my PhD in Mathematical Biology at Memorial University of Newfoundland, where I was supervised by Dr. Amy Hurford. My doctoral work focused on developing and analyzing mechanistic models to study seasonal mosquito dynamics and their implications for vector control.


I work at the intersection of deterministic and stochastic modeling, ecological systems analysis, and climate-data integration. I have expertise in R and LaTeX, working knowledge of Python and its scientific libraries, and experience in scientific computing with Git/GitHub and in macOS and Linux environments.


I also have experience teaching undergraduate mathematics and R programming, and mentoring students in research, data analysis, and scientific communication.


Learn more about my projects and findings in the Research section.

title: Research
text:
Software Development
I developed the climecol R package as part of a broader effort to standardize and streamline climate-data workflows for ecological modeling. The package provides tools for importing, validating, processing, and visualizing climate data, enabling seamless integration of environmental drivers into mechanistic models.
Key features include:

Flexible import and standardization of heterogeneous climate datasets

Data validation and quality control checks

Gap detection and imputation for missing observations

Seasonal fitting and smoothing of temperature and photoperiod

Stochastic sampling of rainfall to preserve seasonal structure

Generation of climate scenarios for simulation studies

The package is designed to promote reproducibility, transparency, and efficiency in climate-driven modeling workflows.
